8: dynamis (dynamis) is not courage or willpower but the divine capacity to perform what exceeds human ability — the same word used for the Spirit's creative power in the Incarnation (Luke 1:35). The promise is not merely empowerment but an endowment that makes the disciples constitutively capable witnesses. martyres (martyres) will become the defining identity-word for believers in Acts (Acts 1:22; Acts 2:32; Acts 3:15; Acts 5:32; Acts 10:39; Acts 13:31; Acts 22:15; Acts 26:16): those who testify to what they have personally seen. The geographic progression — Jerusalem → Judea → Samaria → ends of the earth — is the structural outline of all twenty-eight chapters.
11: ho Galilaioi (hoi Galilaioi): the angelic address specifies their provincial origin; in first-century Judaism, Galileans were regarded with mild condescension by Jerusalem's educated elite. The disciples who will shake the empire are identified as uneducated provincials — Acts' characteristic irony. analepsis (analēpsis) — the same word Luke used in 9:51 for the 'assumption' toward which Jesus set his face — frames the ascension as completion of the entire travel-narrative begun in Luke's central section.
1: ton proton logon (ton prōton logon, 'the first account/word'): the preface ties Acts to Luke's Gospel (proton implies at least a second); logos covers both narrative account and theological word. Theophile (Theophile): the dedicatee appears in Luke 1:1-4 as kratiste (most excellent), a formal address for high-status patrons. Whether Theophilus is a historical patron, a literary figure ('lover of God'), or both, Luke addresses a literate Greco-Roman audience throughout.
2: dia pneumatos hagiou enteila meno (dia pneumatos hagiou enteilamenos, 'having commanded through the Holy Spirit'): the ascension instructions are Spirit-mediated — the risen Christ's final directives are simultaneously the Spirit's directives, anticipating the Spirit's continued voice through the apostolic community in Acts. apostolois (apostolois, 'apostles'): the term emphasizes their commissioned-sender relationship to Christ, not merely discipleship.
3: tekmeriois pollois (tekmēriois pollois, 'many convincing proofs'): tekmērion is a legal term for conclusive evidence (as opposed to sēmeion, sign, or martyria, testimony alone). Luke signals that the resurrection appearances carry evidentiary weight, not merely experiential weight. di' hēmerōn tesserakonta (forty days): the same count as Moses at Sinai (Exodus 24:18), Elijah at Horeb (1 Kings 19:8), and Jesus's wilderness temptation (Luke 4:2); the number marks a divinely structured period of preparation before a new covenant stage.
4: sunalizomenos (sunalizomenos): disputed reading — either 'eating together with' (syn + halizo from hals, salt = sharing salt/eating) or 'assembling together with' (syn + athroizō). The first reading (shared meals) fits Luke's 'eating' resurrection appearances (Luke 24:30, 41-43) and was preferred by Chrysostom. epangelian tou patros (epangelian tou patros, 'the promise of the Father'): the Spirit is pre-promised before being poured out — Acts is the story of a kept promise.
5: en pneumati baptisthesesthe hagio (en pneumati baptisthēsesthe hagiō, 'you will be baptized in the Holy Spirit'): the contrast with John's water-baptism frames Spirit-reception as a distinct, superior act. The passive baptisthēsesthe (you will be baptized) makes God the agent: Pentecost is not something the disciples achieve but something done to them. ou meta pollas tautas hemeras ('not many days from now'): the imminence creates urgency — remain, wait, receive.
6: apokathistaneis ten basileian to Israel (apokathistaneis tēn basileian tō Israēl, 'will you restore the kingdom to Israel?'): apokathistēmi (restore) is the LXX's standard word for Israel's return from exile and covenant restoration (Jeremiah 15:19; Acts 16:15; Hosea 11:11). The disciples ask a legitimate prophetic question — OT promises do include national restoration for Israel. Jesus's answer does not deny the promise but reframes the timing and the community through whom it works.
7: chronous e kairous (chronous ē kairous, 'times or seasons'): chronos refers to measured clock-time; kairos to appointed, decisive moments. The pair covers both calendar-time and moment-time. en te idia exousia (en tē idia exousia, 'by his own authority'): exousia is the freedom of decision that belongs to the Father alone — the same word for Jesus's own authority (Luke 4:36) but here asserted for the Father's sole prerogative over eschatological timing.
9: nephele hypelaben auton (nephelē hypelaben auton, 'a cloud received/took him up'): the cloud is the OT cloud of divine presence (Exodus 13:21; Acts 40:34; 1 Kings 8:10-11). The ascension is not merely departure but entry into the divine presence-sphere. Hypolambanō (receive, take up) is used for a cloud receiving someone only here — the technical vocabulary signals a unique divine act. Luke's eyewitness note ('before their very eyes') grounds the ascension as visible historical event, not purely spiritual experience.
10: duo andres en esthesesi leukais (duo andres en esthēsesi leukais, 'two men in white garments'): the same angelic pair-formula as the transfiguration (Luke 9:30-31, where Moses and Elijah appear alongside Jesus) and the resurrection tomb (Luke 24:4). White garments consistently mark heavenly messengers in apocalyptic literature (Daniel 7:9; Revelation 1:14). The two-witness requirement (Deuteronomy 19:15) may be embedded in the double-messenger pattern.
12: sabbatou echon hodon (sabbatou echon hodon, 'a Sabbath day's journey'): approximately 2,000 cubits (~880 meters), derived from Numbers 35:5's boundary-distance around Levitical cities. Luke's geographic precision grounds the narrative; the Sabbath-distance was not simply 'walking distance' but a legal halakic category — the disciples returned within the permitted limit, observing Torah even as the new age was dawning.
13: to hyperoon (to hypērōon, 'the upper room'): Greek hyperōon (upper story) occurs in Acts at Acts 9:37, 39 and Acts 20:8-9; the upper room is the characteristic space for the Jerusalem community's private meetings. The Twelve minus Judas (now eleven) are listed; James son of Alphaeus and Thaddaeus are included though they nearly disappear from the Acts narrative, replaced by the figure of James the Lord's brother who emerges as the Jerusalem leader by Acts 15.
14: homothumadon te proseuche (homothymadon tē proseuchē, 'with one accord in prayer'): the same homothymadon that will characterize Pentecost (Acts 2:1, 46) begins here with prayer. Prayer is the posture of the community before the Spirit's coming; the sequence — prayer → Spirit → witness — is the structural pattern Acts will repeat. syn gynaixin ('with the women') is significant: the women who witnessed the resurrection (Luke 24) are integral to the praying community, not peripheral to it.
15: en tais hemerais tautais (en tais hēmerais tautais, 'in those days'): Luke's temporal marker for the pre-Pentecost period; the same phrase frames eschatological fulfillment in Peter's Joel-citation (Acts 2:17: 'in the last days'). ochlos onomatōn (ochlos onomatōn, 'a crowd of names/persons'): an unusual Semitism (names = persons, Numbers 1:18, 20 LXX). The 120 may represent the core number required to establish a juridical community in Jewish law (a bet din requires 23; 120 allows for five such courts).
16: edei plērōthenai tēn graphēn (edei plērōthēnai tēn graphēn, 'it was necessary that the Scripture be fulfilled'): the same dei of divine necessity that drove the entire passion narrative (Luke 24:26; Acts 9:22). Peter's hermeneutic: Scripture speaks not merely as historical record but as prophetic directive — Judas's betrayal was foreseen and therefore the vacant apostolic place must be filled. elalasen dia stomatos David ('spoke through the mouth of David'): David as the Spirit's instrument, not the source; the pneumatological understanding of Scripture that 2 Peter 1:21 makes explicit.
17: kateprithmenos en hemin (katērithmenos en hēmin, 'numbered/counted among us'): the verb implies formal membership — Judas was not a marginal follower but an enrolled member of the Twelve with a kleros (lot/share) in the apostolic ministry. ton kleron tes diakonias tautes ('the lot of this ministry'): klēros is both the lot cast to choose and the assigned portion/inheritance; its use here anticipates the lot-casting of Acts 1:26 and confirms that apostolic ministry is a divinely assigned inheritance.
18: misthou tes adikias (misthou tēs adikias, 'wages of wickedness'): misthos (wage, pay) is darkly ironic — Judas received payment for betrayal; his payment purchased the field that became his burial place. The account of his death (falling headlong, bowels burst open) is vivid compared to Matthew's hanging (Acts 27:5); the two accounts are compatible if the body later fell after the rope broke or the body was cut down. Akeldama: an Aramaic transliteration (haqel dema, 'field of blood').
19: gnōston egeneto pasin tois katoikousin Ierousalem ('became known to all the residents of Jerusalem'): Luke's corroborative note that Judas's end was widely known; the field's name preserved the memory publicly. The parenthetical is Luke's narrator-voice (not Peter's speech), explaining for Theophilus's non-Aramaic audience what Akeldama means. The linguistic notation — 'in their language' — is one of Luke's ethnographic markers throughout Acts.
20: en biblo psalmōn (en biblō psalmōn, 'in the book of Psalms'): Peter's two citations (Psalms 69:25 and Psalms 109:8) from the same canonical collection are not random proof-texts but form a hermeneutical case: the psalms of the righteous sufferer betrayed by his companions predict not only the passion but the community's necessary response to the vacant place. Ginetō (let it become) translates the LXX jussive, treated as directive: the Scripture's optative becomes the community's mandate.
21: dei oun ton sunelthonta hemin (dei oun ton synelthonta hēmin, 'it is therefore necessary for one of the men who accompanied us'): dei again — the replacement of Judas is divinely necessary, not merely administratively convenient. The qualification 'who accompanied us throughout the time the Lord Jesus went in and out among us' (eisēlthen kai exēlthen) defines apostleship as eyewitness companionship with the historical Jesus, not just post-resurrection encounter.
22: martys anastaseōs autou (martyra tēs anastaseōs autou, 'a witness of his resurrection'): the resurrection witness is the defining apostolic credential; the criteria of Acts 1:21-22 are not merely selection criteria but a definition of apostolic identity. One who has not been a witness of the resurrection cannot fill the Twelve's function, which is precisely testimony (martyria). This is why Paul's apostleship required the Damascus road appearance (1 Corinthians 9:1; Acts 15:8).
23: estēsan duo Ioseph ton kaloumenon Barsabban (estēsan duo, 'they nominated two'): the community nominates; the Lord chooses (Acts 1:24). The division of human discernment and divine decision is characteristic of Acts' understanding of church governance. Barsabbas (son of the Sabbath) and Matthias are otherwise unknown in the NT; Eusebius (HE 1.12) mentions Matthias as one of the Seventy, but the identification is uncertain.
24: Kardiognosta panton (kardiognōsta pantōn, 'Knower-of-all-hearts'): a compound noun for divine omniscience concentrated in one attribute. The community prays to the one who knows what their own discernment cannot reach — the interior disposition of the two candidates. The same title appears in Acts 15:8 (God the 'heart-knower' bears witness to Gentile faith). The prayer is not passive but specific: 'show us which of these two you have chosen.'
25: ten diakonias kai apostoles (tēn diakonian kai apostolēn, 'the ministry and apostleship'): the pairing of diakonia (service) and apostolē (apostleship) defines the role as both serving and sent — the two dimensions that will characterize the entire Acts mission. idia topon (idion topon, 'his own place'): the place 'belonging to' Judas — an understated phrase for his fate. The contrast with the kleros (inherited lot) of apostolic ministry is sharp.
26: edōkan klērous autois (edōkan klērous autois, 'they gave lots for them'): lot-casting as a decision mechanism has OT precedent (Leviticus 16:8; Joshua 7:14; 1 Chronicles 24:5; Proverbs 16:33) and affirms that the outcome is in God's hands. This is the last lot-casting in the NT; after Pentecost, the Spirit directs the community directly (Acts 13:2; Acts 15:28; Acts 16:6-7). The lot falls to Matthias, completing the restoration of the Twelve as the symbolic foundation-number of the new covenant community.
